step_by_step_thinking:
\boxed{C}
**Clinical Reasoning:** During embryonic development, the kidneys ascend from the posterior abdominal wall toward the lumbar region via the dorsal mesentery. The celiac trunk arises anterior to the developing kidneys and acts as a key landmark; its position prevents normal upward migration of the kidneys due to mechanical obstruction. Fusion of the renal poles in Turner’s syndrome may reflect abnormal descent or arrest, commonly attributed to developmental anomalies influenced by vascular constraints. Among the arteries, only the celiac axis lies proximal enough to obstruct ascending movement before the kidneys reach their typical positions. Thus, disruption of ascent occurs due to proximity and spatial constraint imposed by the celiac artery rather than any lower abdominal vessels like superior/inferior mesenteric arteries or splenic artery.
